sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Global equities advanced as a US-Iran interim peace deal sent crude sharply lower and reignited risk appetite, with mega-cap technology, semiconductors and AI beneficiaries leading. MSCI World approx. +1.5%, MSCI Developed Markets approx. +1.5% and MSCI Emerging Markets approx. +0.5%, the latter restrained by softer Chinese equities. US cash trading closed Thursday for the Juneteenth holiday: the S&P 500 added roughly 1.4% and the Nasdaq Composite about 3.1%, the MAG7 cohort outperforming and the Russell 2000 up 3.9%. Europe was strong, the Euro Stoxx 50 +2.1% and STOXX Europe 600 +3.0% in local terms. In Asia the Nikkei 225 surged roughly 8%, its sharpest week in nearly two years, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng rose around 1.9% and China's CSI 300 edged lower.

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content: US indices closed a volatile week higher as a US-Iran peace deal, announced over the prior weekend, drove crude around 15% lower and lifted risk sentiment, even as leadership narrowed back towards technology. The S&P 500 rose 1.4% and the Nasdaq Composite 3.1%, with gains concentrated in semiconductors and AI beneficiaries; technology led sectors at +4.4% and industrials +3.2%, while energy slumped 5.9% on falling oil. The equal-weight S&P 500 was essentially flat, underscoring thin breadth, though small-caps advanced, the Russell 2000 up 3.9%. The centrepiece was new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's debut FOMC, which held rates at 3.50-3.75% but delivered a hawkish message: the 2026 rate cut was removed from the dot plot, with around half of officials now seeing a hike this year.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content: European equities outperformed, propelled by hopes of Middle East de-escalation and sharply lower oil following the US-Iran peace deal. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 climbed 3.0% in local-currency terms, with Germany's DAX surging 3.92%, the UK's FTSE 100 up 2.66%, France's CAC 40 rising 2.05% and Italy's FTSE MIB adding 0.80%, lifting the Euro Stoxx 50 around 2.1%. Cheaper energy was read as a tailwind for the region's net importers and a salve for the recent inflation impulse, even after the ECB's prior-week rate increase. UK data softened the domestic backdrop, with the unemployment rate rising unexpectedly to 5% in the three months to March and job openings falling to a five-year low, while sterling-sensitive exporters benefited from a weaker pound after the Bank of England's policy decision.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content: APAC markets were mixed but Japan stood out. Japanese equities surged on the US-Iran peace deal and sharply lower oil, a powerful relief for the energy-importing economy: the Nikkei 225 jumped roughly 8%, its strongest week in nearly two years, on an AI-led rally, with the broader TOPIX up about 4%, even as BoJ Governor Ueda's comments raised the odds of a June rate hike. Chinese equities lagged, the CSI 300 drifting lower to its weakest since April despite upbeat trade data, with May exports up 19.4% y/y and imports surging 27.4%, widening the trade surplus to USD 105.4bn, as investors awaited fresh domestic stimulus. Hong Kong's Hang Seng firmed in the regional risk-on mood, while cheaper energy offered relief to Asia ex-Japan net importers and the global technology cycle remained the dominant swing factor for sentiment.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content: The US dollar surged to one-year highs, driven by the US-Iran peace deal and a hawkish debut from Fed Chair Warsh, whose decision to hold rates while signalling that further hikes are more likely widened the rate-differential case for the greenback. EUR/USD fell around 0.4%, unable to draw lasting support from the prior week's ECB hike, while GBP/USD dropped roughly 0.6% following the Bank of England's policy decision. USD/JPY rose about 0.5%, extending gains above 160.00 despite BoJ rate-hike speculation, as the dollar's broad momentum dominated. Warsh's notably shorter statement, light on forward guidance, reinforced a higher-for-longer narrative that underpinned the dollar across the board, leaving the peace-deal risk tone and the repricing of Fed expectations as the week's defining FX drivers.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content: Commodities were dominated by a sharp unwind of the geopolitical risk premium after President Trump signed a US-Iran interim peace deal, with Brent crude sliding below USD 79 and extending its five-day decline to around 16% as shipping began returning to the Strait of Hormuz and an estimated 100mn barrels of stranded Gulf cargoes prepared to sail. Prices found a late floor when planned US-Iran talks in Geneva were cancelled, but the dominant theme remained sharply lower oil and cooling inflation pressure. Gold held a defensive tone, steadying above USD 4,300 ahead of the FOMC as easing energy-driven inflation fears tempered haven demand and a more hawkish Fed lifted real yields. The cross-asset read was clear: cheaper energy supported risk appetite but redirected attention squarely to monetary policy and real yields.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content: Government bonds sold off as the Fed's hawkish hold reset rate expectations. New Chair Warsh's FOMC kept the funds target at 3.50-3.75% but removed the previously projected 2026 cut from the dot plot, lifted inflation forecasts, improved its labour-market assessment and trimmed near-term growth, with about half of officials now pencilling in at least one hike this year. Markets read the message as more hawkish than expected, pushing yields higher and led by the front end: the 2-year UST rose to around 4.21% while the 10-year moved near 4.49%, flattening the curve. The takeaway, in Edward Jones's view, is that rates may stay higher for longer without the Fed yet signalling a renewed tightening cycle. Credit held a firm tone, with IG and HY spreads broadly steady amid the risk-on backdrop.
